coredata.dc:title: Ultra-sensitive turn-on fluorescent sensor based on β-cyclodextrin-stabilized melamine-derived carbon dots for the highly selective dual-detection of dopamine

coredata.dc:description: Dopamine (DA) is a critical catecholamine biomolecule with distinct roles, including regulation of learning and memory, in the human brain. The cost-effective, enzyme-free, and highly stabilized fluorescent sensors for neurotransmitters (e.g., DA) are in high demand. In this regard, the development of turn-on fluorescent sensors has gained significant attention in the research field because of their high signal-to-noise ratio, in contrast to turn-off sensors. In this study, we synthesized blue-fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) from melamine and 3-carboxyphenylboronic acid (3-CPBA) using a solid-state hydrothermal method (MPBCDs) and again modified with β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) by simple magnetic stirring (β-MPBCDs). β-MPBCDs were characterized by UV–Vis, FT-IR, XPS, HRTEM, SEM, XRD, TGA, Raman, and fluorescence measurements. In contrast to the fluorescence of β-MPBCDs alone, the fluorescence of β-MPBCDs combined with guest biomolecule (i.e., pyridoxal: PYL) (β-MPBCDs-PYL), delivered better fluorescence. The host–guest interaction between β-CD and PYL biomolecules within the β-MPBCDs-PYL probe can induce fluorescence sensitivity against DA, which showed a good fluorescence enhancement against DA at different concentrations (0–320 nM) with a lower detection limit of 56 pM. The intracellular sensing and practicality of the probe for DA was investigated in neuroblastoma (SH-SY5Y) cells and biological samples like human serum and cerebrospinal fluid (CSF).

Insets (B and C) show the images of blue-color emissive MPBCDs and β-MPBCDs under UV-illumination (365 nm), and (D and E) PL excitation-dependent emission spectra of MPBCDs and β-MPBCDs under different excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 to 500 nm. (A) PL emission spectra of β-MPBCDs-PYL against various concentration of DA (0–320 nM) (from bottom to top: 0, 5, 10, 20, 40, 80, 160, and 320 nM), (B) Linear relationship plot between (F–F0)/F0 and the concentrations of DA (0–320 nM), (C) Selectivity of β-MPBCDs-PYL for DA sensing in the presence of different interferants. The concentrations of DA and interferants were 320 nM and 1 μM, respectively. (D) UV–Vis absorption spectra of β-MPBCDs, PYL, DA, and β-MPBCDs-PYL-DA.

head.ce:title: Ultra-sensitive turn-on fluorescent sensor based on β-cyclodextrin-stabilized melamine-derived carbon dots for the highly selective dual-detection of dopamine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Gachon University, 1342 Seongnam Daero, Seongnam-Si, Gyeonggi-Do, 13120, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Gachon University, 1342 Seongnam Daero, Seongnam-Si, Gyeonggi-Do, 13120, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dopamine (DA) is a critical catecholamine biomolecule with distinct roles, including regulation of learning and memory, in the human brain. The cost-effective, enzyme-free, and highly stabilized fluorescent sensors for neurotransmitters (e.g., DA) are in high demand. In this regard, the development of turn-on fluorescent sensors has gained significant attention in the research field because of their high signal-to-noise ratio, in contrast to turn-off sensors. In this study, we synthesized blue-fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) from melamine and 3-carboxyphenylboronic acid (3-CPBA) using a solid-state hydrothermal method (MPBCDs) and again modified with β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) by simple magnetic stirring (β-MPBCDs). β-MPBCDs were characterized by UV–Vis, FT-IR, XPS, HRTEM, SEM, XRD, TGA, Raman, and fluorescence measurements. In contrast to the fluorescence of β-MPBCDs alone, the fluorescence of β-MPBCDs combined with guest biomolecule (i.e., pyridoxal: PYL) (β-MPBCDs-PYL), delivered better fluorescence. The host–guest interaction between β-CD and PYL biomolecules within the β-MPBCDs-PYL probe can induce fluorescence sensitivity against DA, which showed a good fluorescence enhancement against DA at different concentrations (0–320 nM) with a lower detection limit of 56 pM. The intracellular sensing and practicality of the probe for DA was investigated in neuroblastoma (SH-SY5Y) cells and biological samples like human serum and cerebrospinal fluid (CSF).

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA) is a catecholamine-based neurotransmitter that is physiologically significant and regulates several cellular processes, including the central nervous system, hormonal system, renal system, and metabolic function [1]. Its abnormality leads to neurological disorders, including Alzheimer’s, Parkinson’s, and Huntington’s diseases, as well as psychological disorders like schizophrenia and depression [2–4]. Thus, sensors with superior sensitivity and high specificity are required for DA detection. Several detection approaches have been developed, including high-performance liquid chromatography-coupled mass spectrometry, capillary electrophoresis, chemiluminescence, electrochemical methods, colorimetry, and surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) [5–10]. Unfortunately, these methods are not satisfactory for the DA detection because they are neither cost-effective nor time-saving strategies. In contrast, nanomaterials that utilize the fluorescence principle are highly sensitive with suitable biocompatibility, robust sensitivity, and desired photochemical properties; they are less costly and work within a short time [11,12].

Currently, the use of carbon dots (CDs), which are in a highly desirable ultra-nano size form (<10 nm) for fluorescence-based sensors has gained wide acceptance for their unique fluorescence emission properties, ultra-level morphology, fluorescence tuning ability, non-toxicity, biodegradability, and potential surface modification [13]. Furthermore, CDs with hetero atom doping (e.g., N, B, and S) exhibit high quantum yield, high fluorescence emission, and chemical stability, and are non-toxic [14–17]. It is noteworthy that a solid-state synthesis approach can open new possibilities for the development of solvent-free CDs without the use of harsh chemicals. Few reports are available based on this approach, which retains the dispersible CDs with a sufficient morphological structure. For example, Iqbal et al. developed a fluorescent turn-off probe for the detection of Fe3+ and Fe2+ ions through solid-state synthesis using citric acid and 1,10-phenanthroline [18]. Consequently, the probe was found to be dispersible with good cellular uptake and can therefore be used for cell imaging. In another study, researchers prepared nitrogen-doped CDs by solid-state synthesis from precursors such as melamine and citric acid and further adapted them for turn-off-on mercury (Hg2+) and glutathione (GSH) detection and cell imaging investigations [19]. However, the chemical stability of the CDs during the surface-functionalization process is crucial for their enhanced sensitivity without compromising their fluorescence response. In this context, β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) is an exceptional biomolecule that is used to stabilize the structural integrity of various nanomaterials, such as gold nanoclusters (Au NCs), gold nanoparticles (Au NPs), and CDs. They consist of external and internal cavities in their molecular structure, which are hydrophilic and hydrophobic in nature, and thereby can be utilized as selective host biomolecules [20]. Recently, these biomolecules have been employed in sensing, theranostics, and drug delivery by utilizing their promising host–guest interactions with different analytes [21-25]. Halawa et al. designed β-CD functionalized Au NCs, which showed fluorescence quenching for DA sensing based on the selective host–guest interaction [26]. Their quenching mechanism was confirmed through the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process. A colorimetric probe was developed using β-CD modified Au NPs for DA sensing, where assembled Au nanostructures were formed based on the induced concentrations of DA [27]. Although both sensors showed a reasonable sensitivity of around 20 nM, further research is still required to evaluate their sensing abilities. Meanwhile, several other sensors have also been developed using β-CD as a stabilizing agent for the host-guest interaction in detecting diverged analytes [28–31]. Unfortunately, none of these sensors have been investigated for in vitro applications.

Pyridoxal (PYL) is one of the active biomolecular forms of vitamin B6 and is applicable as a cofactor for a wide range of physiological activities in the human body [32]. Its hydrophilicity is derived from the pyridine core and carbonyl (CO) and hydroxyl (-OH) functional groups, which can act as suitable guest molecules adsorbed over the external cavity of the host, that is, β-CD. For instance, researchers developed a PYL-based fluorescence turn-on pH sensor by combining aggregation-induced emission (AIE) and excited-state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT) [33]. It is essential to note that the fluorescence behavior of the developed sensor under protonation, deprotonation, and aggregation conditions can be used to determine acidic, alkaline, and physiological pH levels. In another study, researchers constructed a highly sensitive molecular sensor using PYL, based on the “turn on fluorescence” principle, for detecting zinc (Zn2+) metal ions. The sensor demonstrated intracellular sensing potentials in HeLa cells [34]. There is also another cofactor of vitamin B6 called pyridoxal 5-phosphate (PLP), which is used as a specific cofactor for the detection of biologically important enzymes such as alkaline phosphatase (ALP) [35].

A variety of boronic acid-modified sensors have been developed recently using 3-aminophenylboronic acid (APBA) and 3-mercaptophenylboronic acid (3-MPBA) as well-known ligands of interest for DA sensing [36,37]. Despite their impressive sensitivity, their preparation and optimization methods were lengthy and complex. In addition, several fluorescent sensors have been proposed for sensing DA, which are based on the physical and chemical reactions of the enzyme and CDs [38–40]. The above sensors, however, correspond to the fluorescence “turn-off” approach, which provides comparatively more false positive signals than fluorescence “turn-on” sensors [41]. Based on the specific interaction of cis-diols with boronic acid moieties, and host-guest interaction between the β-CD and PYL, we developed a fluorescent “turn-on” sensor using β-MPBCDs, which can selectively detect DA among similar interferants.

In this study, melamine and 3-carboxyphenylboronic acid (3-CPBA) were chosen as selective precursors for synthesizing highly sensitive nitrogen and boron-doped CDs (MPBCDs) using a straightforward solid-state hydrothermal method. The prepared blue-fluorescent MPBCDs were stabilized with β-CD to synthesize β-MPBCDs through simple magnetic stirring. The β-MPBCDs showed excitation-dependent emission, biocompatibility, and non-toxicity properties. The fluorescence emission of β-MPBCDs was enhanced after adding PYL and the resulting β-MPBCDs-PYL probe showed fluorescence enhancement towards DA at different concentrations.

Melamine, β-cyclodextrin (β-CD), pyridoxal (PYL), dopamine hydrochloride (DA), 3-carboxyphenylboronic acid (3-CPBA), γ-aminobutyric acid (GABA), epinephrine (EP), norepinephrine (NE), serotonin (5-HT), potassium chloride (KCl), magnesium nitrate Mg(NO3)2, sodium chloride (NaCl), calcium chloride (CaCl2), glucose (Glu), glutathione (GSH), uric acid (UA), l-cysteine (L-Cys), ascorbic acid (AA), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), and l-glutamic acid were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). A dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cutoff (MWCO): 100–500 D was acquired from Spectrum Laboratories Inc. Deionized (DI) water was used in the experiments.

The UV–visible spectra of the MPBCDs and β-MPBCDs were measured using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Varian Cary 100). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured using a fluorescence spectrometer (Quanta Master, Photon Technology International, USA) with a monochromator, xenon lamp (Arc Lamp Housing: A-1010B™), and power supply (Brytexbox). The fluorescence lifetime decay experiment was carried out using the Photon Technology International EasyLife II fluorometer system and an auto-adjustable excitation wavelength between 260 and 650 nm. The pH was calibrated using a Thermo Scientific pH meter. A Bruker Vertex high-resolution 70 FT-IR spectrometer was used to obtain the FT-IR spectra. An X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (K-AlphaTM, Thermo Scientific) was used to measure the XPS spectra of the β-MPBCDs. Raman spectra of β-MPBCDs were recorded by employing micro-Raman spectroscopy (ANDOR Monora500i, 633 nm). X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was investigated using Rigaku ultima III X-ray diffractometer equipped with Cu sealed tube of λ = 1.54,178 Å, 40 kV, 30 mA, the scan speed of 2°/min in continuous scanning 2θ mode. Thermal stability studies for the β-MPBCDs were performed using the SDT Q600 (V20.9 Build 20) (TGA-DTA) analyzer under N2 (heating rate: 10 °C min−1, flow rate: of 100 mL min−1. TEM and HRTEM images of the β-MPBCDs were obtained using a Tecnai G2 F20S-TWIN 200 kV electron microscope. Intracellular sensing experiments were performed using β-MPBCDs in SH-SY5Y cells using a fluorescence microscope.

MPBCDs were prepared by a solid-state hydrothermal method using melamine and 3-CPBA without any solvents. Prior to the experiment, all glassware was cleaned with aqua regia (HCl:HNO3 = 3:1, v/v). In a typical synthesis, 1.26 g of melamine and 2.5 g of 3-CPBA were thoroughly mixed in a beaker and placed in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (50 mL). Further, the autoclave was heated at 180 °C for 6 h in a hot-air oven, and the obtained powder was kept aside. The as-prepared MPBCDs were thoroughly rinsed, washed three times with DI water, and centrifuged (10,000 rpm for 30 min). Again, the MPBCDs were dialyzed against DI water for 24 h using a dialysis membrane (MWCO: 100–500 D) to remove impurities. The purified MPBCDs were dissolved in 100 mL DI water for further functionalization.

β-MPBCDs were synthesized using MPBCDs by simple magnetic stirring. For this reaction, β-CD (0.5 g) and MPBCDs (20 mL) were thoroughly mixed to obtain a uniform solution. This mixture was then dissolved in 25 mL of PBS (10 mM; pH 7.4) and mixed homogenously using magnetic stirring for 12 h. Subsequently, a white-colored solution was obtained, which was further centrifuged (10,000 rpm) for 30 min to remove unreacted and larger impurities (i.e., β-MPBCDs). The obtained supernatant solution consisting of β-MPBCDs was dialyzed for 24 h using a dialysis membrane (MWCO: 100–500 D) and filtered through a syringe filter (0.22 μm), and β-MPBCDs were freeze-dried and later refrigerated for further analysis. The corresponding synthesis and functionalization steps are illustrated in Scheme 1 .

In a typical run, 200 μL of β-MPBCDs (2.0 mg mL−1) and 200 μL of PYL (10 mM) were added to a cuvette containing 500 μL of PBS (10 mM; pH 7.4) and DI water (2 mL). To this cuvette, different concentrations of DA (0–320 nM) were added and incubated for 15 min, and the PL spectra were measured using an excitation wavelength of 357 nm.

Neuroblastoma cells (SH-SY5Y) were cultured according to the American Type Culture Collection (ATCC) guidelines. A mixture of 1% penicillin-streptomycin with 15% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and Dulbecco’s Minimum Essential Medium (DMEM) were used for the cell culture. The SH-SY5Y cells were cultured and further incubated under a 5% CO2 atmosphere at 37 °C. Subsequently, SH-SY5Y cells were further sub-cultured once the optimal confluency (80–90%) was reached.

In a six-well plate, SH-SY5Y cells were cultured and maintained at confluency (80–90%) by maintaining suitable incubation conditions at 37 °C and 5% CO2 for 24 h. Immediately, the medium was discarded, fresh medium was changed by employing different concentrations of β-MPBCDs-PYL probe (0, 40, 80, 120, 160, and 200 μg. mL−1), and they were further incubated for 48 h. Subsequently, the MTT reagent was added, and SH-SY5Y cells were incubated for 3 h. Finally, a microplate reader was used to measure the absorbance at 570 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular detection of DA using β-MPBCDs-PYL probe
ce:section.ce:para: The SH-SY5Y cells were cultured in a six-well plate over the coverslips with the aid of the essential medium and later incubated (37 °C, 5% CO2 for 24 h). Next, the medium was removed, and Dulbecco’s phosphate-buffered saline (DPBS) was used to rinse the cells and thoroughly remove the excess medium. β-MPBCDs-PYL probe (100 μg. mL−1) was used as the control and incubated with SH-SY5Y cells. Then, different concentrations of DA (80 and 320 nM) were added to the above mixture, and the cells were maintained at optimal incubation conditions (37 °C, 5% CO2 for 1 h). Finally, the coverslips were washed three times with DPBS, and cell images were acquired under a fluorescent microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of DA in human biological samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practicality of the β-MPBCDs-PYL probe (100 μg. mL−1) was validated in human serum and cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) to determine DA. First, sample pretreatment was performed for human serum and CSF following a previously reported method [42]. Serum and CSF samples were centrifuged (8000 rpm, 30 min), acetonitrile (0.5 mL) was added to the obtained supernatant solutions, and the proteins were precipitated. Subsequently, supernatants were centrifuged again (8000 rpm, 30 min), and the serum and CSF samples were diluted 100-fold using PBS (10 mM, pH 7.4). Spiked samples were prepared using different concentrations of DA (5, 20, 40, 80, and 320 nM). Finally, these spiked samples were employed for the determination of DA using the β-MPBCDs-PYL probe.

The morphology, crystallinity, and structural bonding of the β-MPBCDs were analyzed by TEM, HRTEM, XRD, and Raman spectroscopy. The TEM images show a spherical morphology with a distinguishable aggregation, which is due to the shielding of the β-CD phase surrounding the β-MPBCDs ( Fig. 1 A-C). The inset TEM image of the β-MPBCDs shows the particle size distribution curve, which revealed an average particle size of 8 nm, as shown in Fig. 1B. Distinguishable lattice fringes were observed in the HRTEM images of β-MPBCDs, which show a lattice plane interspacing of 0.24 nm, representing their crystallinity (Fig. 1D) [20,43]. Again, the TEM and HRTEM images of MPBCDs have shown uniform dispersion with spherical morphology (Figs. S1A–C). Also, the inset HRTEM image of MPBCDs has revealed lattice fringes, which confirms the crystallinity (Fig. S1C). The average particle size was found to be 5 nm based on the Gaussian particle size histogram (Fig. S1D). Moreover, the SEM elemental mapping of the β-MPBCDs demonstrated the presence of the following elements: carbon (C), oxygen (O), nitrogen (N), and boron (B), as shown in Fig. S2A-D. The corresponding SEM image is shown in Fig. S2E. The existence of the above-mentioned elements can be confirmed by the EDX spectrum of the β-MPBCDs as depicted in Fig. S3. Further, to investigate the crystalline nature of the β-MPBCDs, we studied their XRD patterns, which showed multiple distinct sharp peaks, as shown in Fig. 2 A [44]. The peaks can be correlated with the XRD planes of melamine (JCPDS card no. 24–1654) and were assigned to crystal planes (110), (011), (111), (210), (310), (220), and (311) [45]. It indicates that melamine reacted sufficiently with β-CD during the formation of β-MPBCDs. Also, the XRD pattern of β-MPBCDs demonstrated a weak diffraction peak at 26.6° (2θ), which is consistent with the typical peak (002) of graphitic carbon [46]. We have studied the XRD pattern of MPBCDs alone to compare the XRD results of β-MPBCDs ( Fig. S4). The obtained XRD pattern has revealed the crystalline nature of MPBCDs with multiple sharp peaks. These results were consistent with the XRD pattern of melamine [45]. Also, these results agree with the HRTEM observations of MPBCDs. Based on the above findings, it has been suggested that the β-CD-stabilization over the MPBCDs (i.e., β-MPBCDs) does not alter the crystallinity of final β-MPBCDs. These results suggest that the prepared β-MPBCDs are crystalline, which is corroborated by the HRTEM results. Fig. 2B shows the Raman spectra of the β-MPBCDs, which revealed two bands, namely D (sp3 hybridized carbons) and G (sp2 hybridized carbons) bands at 1363 and 1534 cm−1, respectively [47]. It is noteworthy that the low (IG/ID) intensity ratio of below 1 for the Raman spectra of the β-MPBCDs suggests the availability of slightly formed graphitic structures, and these results are consistent with the HRTEM and XRD results [48]. Fig. 2C shows the TGA analysis of the β-MPBCDs, which were investigated in the temperature range of 0 and 700 °C. Firstly, the degradation of MPBCDs began around 120 °C (green line) due to moisture loss, and they remain stable up to nearly 300 °C (green line) [49]. However, the degradation of β-MPBCDs is rapid at further higher temperatures, which is due to the loss of amine and boronic acid groups and a final weight loss of 69.5% is observed. The moderate thermal stability of the β-MPBCDs is attributed to the degradation of the β-CD structure [50].

Fig. 2D shows the FT-IR spectrum of the prepared β-MPBCDs, which reveals different typical functional groups of β-CD. A broad band and another small band were observed in the FT-IR spectra of β-MPBCDs at 3225 and 1643 cm−1, which correspond to the –OH stretching and asymmetric stretching vibrations, respectively [51]. The two bands at 1023 and 1073 cm−1 correspond to the (C–O) and (C–C) stretching vibrations, respectively. The band at 1150 cm−1 indicates (C–O–C) glycosidic stretching vibrations [52]. The band at 2928 cm−1 is attributed to asymmetric (C–H) stretching vibrations. The multiple bands at 1331–1413 cm−1 are responsible for (C–O) and (C–H) bending vibrations. The band at 1304 cm−1 is responsible for the bending vibrations of (B–O) groups. The two bands at 1094 and 1196 cm−1 are attributed to the (C–N) stretching vibrations. The other bands at 1578 and 1604 cm−1 are accredited to the (-NH) bending vibrations. The band at 1691 cm−1 is assigned to the (CO) stretching vibrations. The obtained FT-IR spectra of the β-MPBCDs share typical spectral characteristics from the FT-IR spectra of β-CD and MPBCDs [53]. To compare these results, we have investigated the FT-IR studies in β-CD and MPBCDs (Fig. S5). The FT-IR spectra of β-CD alone have shown the characteristic bands at 3312 and 2916 cm−1, which are responsible for the (O–H) and (C–H) stretching vibrations, respectively (Fig. S5A). There were different bands between 1333 and 1414 cm−1, ascribed to the (C–O) and (C–H) bending vibrations. The glycosidic (C–O–C) stretching vibrations were observed at 1152 cm−1. The other bands at 1021, 1079, and 1641 cm−1 were responsible for the (C–O), (C–C), and (O–H) stretching vibrations, respectively [21]. These results indicate that β-CD has successfully stabilized on the surface of native MPBCDs. Again, the FT-IR spectra of MPBCDs have shown typical stretching vibrations of the triazine ring from the melamine moiety at 583, 1452, 1485, and 1555 cm−1 (Fig. S5B). Also, the (NH2) stretching vibrations from the melamine were observed in the FT-IR spectra of MPBCDs at 3105, 3329, and 3493 cm−1 [54]. The bands at 1071 and 1205 cm−1 are responsible for the (C–N) stretching vibrations. The bands at 1586 and 1295 cm−1 were responsible for the vibrations of a new benzene ring and (B–O) bending vibrations of 3-CPBA moieties, respectively [55]. The peaks at 1017, 1158, and 1684 cm−1 assigning to (C–O), (C–O–C), and (CO) stretching vibrations, respectively. Based on these results, it has been suggested that precursors like melamine and 3-CPBA reacted while forming MPBCDs.

The chemical environment of the β-MPBCDs was investigated using XPS. Fig. 3 A shows the total survey spectra of the β-MPBCDs with C 1s, O 1s, N 1s, and B 1s at 285.48, 532.19, 398.98, and 192.83 eV, respectively. The high-resolution C 1s spectra show deconvolutions around 283.52, 285.19, and 286.86 eV with (CC/C–C), (C–N/C–O), and (CO) groups, respectively (Fig. 3B) [56]. The high-resolution spectra of O 1s exhibited deconvolutions at 529.83, 531.24, and 532.83 eV, which ascribed to (C–O), (CO), and (C–OH), respectively, as shown in Fig. 3C [57]. Fig. 3D shows the high-resolution spectra of N 1s, which consists of a single peak at 398.21 eV representing N–H bonding. After the functionalization of 3-CPBA to the β-MPBCDs, the high-resolution spectrum of B 1s showed a single main deconvolution around 191.53 eV, as shown in Fig. 3E [58–60].

The optical properties of the MPBCDs and β-MPBCDs were investigated using UV–Vis and fluorescence methods. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of MPBCDs showed a small broad peak at approximately 280 nm, which represents the π-π* transition (Fig. 4 A). The obtained results were similar to the UV–Vis absorption spectra of previously prepared melamine CDs. However, in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the β-MPBCDs, a visible peak was observed at 270 nm, corresponding to the π–π * transition. These results suggest that the native MPBCDs were well-stabilized by β-CD. The PL excitation and emission spectra of the MPBCDs showed maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of 357 and 459 nm, respectively, as shown in Fig. 4B. The fluorescence behavior was not altered even in the PL spectra of the β-MPBCDs, which showed similar maximum excitation and emission wavelengths (Fig. 4C). These results confirm that the optical properties of the β-MPBCDs were not affected during the functionalization reaction. Both the MPBCDs and β-MPBCDs displayed blue emission under UV light illumination (365 nm). However, a slight increase in fluorescence intensity was observed for the β-MPBCDs (inset, Fig. 4B and C). Excitation-dependent emission behavior was observed in the PL emission spectra of MPBCDs and β-MPBCDs under different excitation wavelengths changing from 300 to 500 nm (at 20 nm intervals) (Fig. 4D and E). A similar phenomenon has been observed with other CDs functionalized with β-CD, which is an ideal property for in vitro bio-imaging studies [21,22]. The quantum yields of the MPBCDs and β-MPBCDs were calculated as 8.3% and 10.9%, respectively. As for the quantum yield value, it is comparable to that of similar β-CD functionalized CDs [23,51]. The modification of β-CD within the MPBCDs may result in high fluorescence and quantum yield for the β-MPBCDs [20]. In addition, to improve the fluorescence sensing of DA, we used PYL along with the prepared β-MPBCDs. The PL emission spectrum of the PYL cofactor (10 mM) alone showed an emission maximum at approximately 459 nm (Fig. 5 A). Thus, it is evident that the PYL is a fluorescent molecule that can enhance PL emission properties of nanomaterials. For a detailed understanding, we measured the PL emission for the β-MPBCDs alone and the β-MPBCDs with PYL. The PL emission spectrum of the β-MPBCDs alone showed an emission maximum at approximately 459 nm (curve i; Fig. 5B). However, after adding PYL to these β-MPBCDs, there was an enhancement in the PL intensity without changing the PL emission behavior (curve ii; Fig. 5B). Based on these observations, we chose PYL as a guest molecule for the selected host, that is, β-CD, which can form the host-guest interactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of DA using β-MPBCDs-PYL probe
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing performance of the β-MPBCDs-PYL probe for DA detection was investigated by measuring the fluorescence spectra. For this purpose, we performed PL spectroscopy using β-MPBCDs-PYL as fluorescence probes against DA at different concentrations, as shown in Fig. 6 A. Fluorescence enhancement of β-MPBCDs-PYL was observed depending on the concentration (0–320 nM) of DA (from bottom to top: 0, 5, 10, 20, 40, 80, 160, and 320 nM). Fig. 6B shows the linear regression graph, which was plotted between [(F–F0)/F0] and DA concentration (0–320 nM), with a correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.9755. Here, F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of β-MPBCDs-PYL before and after the addition of DA, respectively. High sensitivity was shown by the β-MPBCDs-PYL in the linear concentration range of 0–320 nM, which delivered a low limit of detection (LOD) of 56 pM. The obtained LOD was calculated using equation 3σ/k, where σ and k represent the standard deviation and the slope of the regression graph, respectively. It is noteworthy that the sensitivity of the proposed sensor is highly significant for biological applications and qualifies as a fluorescent probe because of the optimal concentrations (0.01–1 μM) of DA available in the human brain. For a better comparison, we summarized the sensitivity aspects of earlier reported analytical probes with the proposed β-MPBCDs-PYL probe as shown in Table 1 [7,11,27,31,36,39,40,53,56,61–64]. In contrast, the proposed sensor has improved DA detection limits by nearly two orders of magnitude.

The selectivity of the β-MPBCDs and β-MPBCDs-PYL probe was investigated by measuring the fluorescence spectra against DA using various interferants (Fig. 6C). Different metal ions (K+, Na+, Mg2+, and Ca2+), major biomolecules (UA, AA, Glu, L-Glu, GSH, and L-Cys), and other neurotransmitters (EP, NE, 5-HT, and GABA) were used. To understand complete interference, high concentrations (1 μM) of interferants compared to that of DA (320 nM) were used. A significant fluorescence enhancement in the β-MPBCDs-PYL probe was observed compared to the fluorescence of the interferants. This suggests that the fluorescence of the proposed probe was not disturbed even in the presence of a complex matrix consisting of miscellaneous interferants. The high sensitivity and selectivity of the proposed probe for DA may depend on two strong interactions (Scheme 2 ): i) the interaction between the cis-diol groups of DA and the boronic acid moieties, and ii) the imine bond (Schiff base) formation between the amine (NH2) group of DA and the aldehyde (-CHO) group of PYL. This interaction possibly induces the delocalization of electrons around the probe structure, which activates the “turn on” signal for the detection of DA. To confirm the sensing mechanism of the proposed probe, we performed a fluorescence lifetime decay analysis (Fig. S6). The fitting of the decay curves was followed by employing the double exponential model according to the following equation:

Here, D, τ1 and τ2, and A1 and A2 are the PL decay, lifetime, and pre-exponential factors, respectively. The lifetimes τ1 and τ2 were observed around 10.26 ns and 0.021 ns, respectively, for the β-MPBCDs-PYL alone. Interestingly, after the addition of DA to the β-MPBCDs-PYL probe, their lifetimes τ1 and τ2 were increased to 14.54 ns and 0.030 ns, respectively. These results suggest that the lower fluorescence intensity of the β-MPBCDs-PYL probe was due to electron deficiency, whereas after adding DA to the β-MPBCDs-PYL probe, fluorescence enhancement occurred due to the delocalization of electrons. Further confirmation was obtained by comparing the UV–Vis absorbance bands of the various compounds, such as β-MPBCDs, PYL, and DA alone, with those of the β-MPBCDs-PYL probe treated DA (320 nM) (Fig. 6D). Typical absorption bands in the UV–Vis spectrum of PYL were observed at 259, 289, and 316 nm. The two absorption bands at 259 and 316 nm did not shift even after the addition of DA to the probe. As a result of the interaction between PYL and DA on the surface of the β-MPBCDs-PYL probe, the absorption peak at 289 nm was blue-shifted to 284 nm. In addition, TEM images of the β-MPBCDs-PYL probe after the treatment of DA (320 nM) demonstrated that the morphology of the probe has not altered by the DA molecule (Fig. S7). These findings strongly support the previously developed boronic acid CDs [64]. The above discussions imply that the proposed probe is highly significant for DA sensing.
The fluorescence emission of the β-MPBCDs-PYL probe was studied under extreme conditions.

The probe delivered maximum fluorescence intensity at a biological pH of approximately 7, which is an ideal property that reflects the physiological environment (Fig. S8A). However, under acidic and alkaline conditions, distinct fluctuations in the fluorescence intensities were observed. This property may depend on the presence of various hydrophilic functional groups such as –OH, CO, and C–O. Fig. S8B shows the fluorescence intensity of the β-MPBCDs-PYL probe at different molar concentrations of KCl. It’s interesting to note that there were no fluctuations in fluorescence intensity, indicating the ionic stability of the β-MPBCDs-PYL probe. Additionally, the fluorescence intensity of the probe did not change throughout the course of the 180-day period, demonstrating the effectiveness of its fluorescence emission (Fig. S8C ). After being treated to DA (320 nM) for two months, the fluorescence stability of the β-MPBCDs-PYL probe was examined using PL measurements (Fig. S9). It has been observed that the fluorescence intensity of the probe alone has decreased in contrast to the fluorescence intensity of the probe treated with DA, which displayed increased fluorescence. According to the findings, the probe exhibits a high sensitivity and long-lasting steady response to DA.

The cytotoxicity of β-MPBCDs-PYL probe was investigated by MTT assay using SH-SY5Y cells as an in vitro model. Adequate cytotoxicity was observed in cells after incubation with the β-MPBCDs-PYL probe for 48 h at different concentrations (0, 40, 80, 120, 160, and 200 μg. mL−1) (Fig. S10). Cell viability was dependent on the concentration of the β-MPBCDs-PYL probe used in SH-SY5Y cells. When the concentration of β-MPBCDs-PYL was increased, a significant decrease in cell viability was observed in SH-SY5Y cells. These results suggest that the proposed probe has measurable cytotoxic effects on the cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro sensing studies of β-MPBCDs-PYL probe
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed β-MPBCDs-PYL could be used as a suitable fluorescence sensing probe for intracellular DA sensing. Fig. 7 A shows fluorescence microscopy images of β-MPBCDs-PYL-treated SH-SY5Y cells without treatment with DA (control). To better compare the results, two individual concentrations of DA (80 and 320 nM) were treated with β-MPBCDs-PYL and analyzed again using a fluorescence microscope, as shown in Fig. 7B and C. Adequate fluorescence enhancement was observed for the β-MPBCDs-PYL probe. This confirmed that the proposed probe is suitable as a fluorescent sensing probe for DA detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DA in human biological samples
ce:section.ce:para:
The practicality of the β-MPBCDs-PYL probe (100 μg. mL−1) was investigated for the determination of DA in biological samples, such as human serum and CSF. Spiked samples were prepared in human serum and CSF using various concentrations of DA (5, 20, 40, 80, and 320 nM). Tables 2 and 3
depict their recovery values in the ranges of 95.1–102.4% and 95.4–104.6% for human serum and CSF, respectively. The relative standard deviation (RSD) values for the human serum and CSF were in the ranges of 2.26–5.31% and 1.37–5.86%, respectively. These results suggest the feasibility of using the developed sensor for DA detection in biological samples.

In this study, we developed a turn-on fluorescent sensor using host-guest interactions between β-CD and PYL. We synthesized nitrogen- and boron-doped CDs (MPBCDs) using melamine and 3-CPBA via a solid-state hydrothermal reaction. The resulting MPBCDs were stabilized with β-CD via a facile magnetic stirring reaction (β-MPBCDs). PYL is a fluorescent biomolecule that can be used as a potential guest compound for the prepared β-MPBCDs, and the resulting β-MPBCDs-PYL probe was utilized for DA sensing. Herein, two strong interactions may explain the high sensitivity of the probe for DA. The first occurs due to the interaction between the cis-diol groups of DA and the boronic acid moieties, and the second due to the formation of imine bonds between the –NH2 and –CHO moieties of DA and PYL, respectively. The β-MPBCDs-PYL probe demonstrated adequate fluorescence enhancement towards DA in the range (0–320 nM) with a LOD of 56 pM. By using SH-SY5Y cells as in vitro models, the probe was examined for its intracellular sensing performance in DA (80 and 320 nM). The lifetime decay and UV–Vis analysis of the β-MPBCDs-PYL probe provided a better understanding of its ability to detect DA. In addition, the probe was validated in human biological samples, such as serum and CSF samples, and showed outstanding recovery percentage ranges (95.1–102.4% and 95.4–104.6%). Nonetheless, the fluorescence stability of the probe was maintained in media with different pH values and ionic (KCl) concentrations over an extended time period. This study offers a promising cost-effective method for DA determination without the need for enzymes. However, the low-wavelength fluorescence emission of the proposed probe could be an obstacle in avoiding interference from biological compounds during in vivo studies. Hence, in the future, the long-wavelength emission needs to be tuned for β-CD-stabilized nitrogen-doped CDs to obtain sufficient results for in vivo applications.
